content.assessment: As an explicit no-new-policy counterfactual, this proposal is the clearest illustration of the lens's causal-attribution caution: any observed change in track share or size under this scenario would be attributable to demographic mechanics alone, not to any equity or quality intervention, and thus cannot be interpreted as evidence for or against reform efficacy. Critically, the evidence indicates that passive contraction is likely to be geographically uneven, potentially worsening rather than improving equity by preserving urban flagship selectivity while eroding only marginal rural provision, and it does nothing to address the SES sorting or ethnic/regional equity gaps documented independently of track structure.
opportunities: Provides a genuinely necessary evidentiary baseline for isolating the causal effect of any of the other four proposals, addressing the lens's core attribution concern directly
Highlights that demographic contraction is already reshaping the network irrespective of policy choice, which could inform sequencing of active reforms
strengths: Serves a clear methodological function as a counterfactual baseline against which the causal contribution of active reforms can be assessed
Correctly identifies a real, structural mechanism (statutory class-size minimums interacting with cohort decline) that will mechanically affect network viability regardless of policy choice
threats: Uneven geographic contraction could concentrate selectivity in urban flagships while eroding rural access, worsening geographic equity even as aggregate selective-track share falls nationally
Church/private schools with different demographic exposure could gain relative share, altering sector composition in ways outside public planning intent and independent of any equity objective
weaknesses: Does nothing to address the SES/family-capital sorting mechanism, which the evidence indicates continues unabated among any remaining capacity
Does not address the substantial, independently documented Roma/regional early-school-leaving equity gap, which is unrelated to gimnázium track share and would persist unchanged
